step.title: Step 45
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> RESET

sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: GAME OVER triggered a reset! The right box shrank back to 12 pixels, bottom box reappeared. Player is back below the bottom box at rows 44-47, cols 32-35. The game reset to a similar initial state. I need to understand the correct sequence to grow all boxes without consuming them.
- Plan: First, let me examine the current state and re-examine the right box interaction to understand the exact SPACE behavior.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 201 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.8x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 201 actions returned the board to a state already seen 201 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 201 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the current state clearly:
- Right box: c, 12 pixels, rows 24-27, cols 44-47
- Left box: c, 12 pixels, rows 28-31, cols 16-19
- Bottom box: c, 12 pixels, rows 36-39, cols 32-35
- Center bar: b, 28 pixels, rows 28-31, cols 28-39 (with g interior)
- Strip: W, 4 pixels, rows 48, cols 32-35
- Player: N, 12 pixels, rows 49-51, cols 32-35
And from the right box interaction:
- H27 (SPACE): strip at rows 32, cols 44-47 (4 pixels) - strip didn't extend!
- H28 (SPACE): strip at rows 28-32, cols 44-47 (16 pixels) - strip extended!
- H29 (SPACE): strip at rows 32, cols 44-47 (4 pixels) - strip retracted!
- H30 (LEFT): strip at rows 32-35, cols 40 (4 pixels) - strip moved with player
So SPACE toggles the strip between extended and retracted states! When extended, the strip reaches up to the box's position. When the strip is extended and LEFT is pressed, the box merges/grows.
Wait, let me re-examine. At H27, the strip was at rows 32, cols 44-47 (4 pixels). The right box was at rows 24-27. The strip didn't reach the box.
At H28, the strip extended to rows 28-32, cols 44-47 (16 pixels). The strip still didn't reach the box (rows 24-27).
At H29, the strip retracted to rows 32, cols 44-47 (4 pixels).
At H30, LEFT was pressed, and the box grew.
But wait, at H27, the box changed from c to G and shifted to rows 28-31. At H28, the box changed back to c at rows 24-27. At H29, the box changed to G at rows 28-31. At H30, LEFT merged them.
So the sequence was:
- H27: SPACE → box became G (rows 28-31), strip at rows 32 (4 pixels)
- H28: SPACE → box became c (rows 24-27), strip extended to rows 28-32 (16 pixels)
- H29: SPACE → box became G (rows 28-31), strip retracted to rows 32 (4 pixels)
- H30: LEFT → box merged to 24 pixels (rows 24-31)
So SPACE alternates between:
1. Toggle box to G state (shifted position), strip stays at 4 pixels
2. Toggle box back to c state, strip extends to 16 pixels
3. Toggle box to G state, strip retracts to 4 pixels
4. LEFT merges when box is in G state
The key insight: SPACE toggles the box between c and G states. When the box is in G state, LEFT merges it. The strip's extension/retraction is a side effect.
Now for the bottom box:
- H2 (UP): box became G (rows 36-39, same position)
- H4 (SPACE): box disappeared, strip grew to 16 pixels (rows 36-40)
The bottom box's G state was at the SAME position (rows 36-39), not shifted. And SPACE consumed it.
Why the difference? For the right box, the G state shifted DOWN (from rows 24-27 to 28-31). For the bottom box, the G state stayed at rows 36-39.
Maybe the shift direction depends on the player's position. For the right box, the player was below the box, and the G state shifted DOWN (toward the player). For the bottom box, the player was also below the box, but the G state stayed in place.
Wait, actually for the bottom box, the G state might have shifted DOWN too, but there was no room (the player was below). So the G state stayed at the same position.
Actually, looking at the bottom box's G state: it was at rows 36-39, same as the original. If it shifted DOWN, it would be at rows 40-43, but the player was at rows 41-43 at that time. So maybe the shift was blocked by the player.
Hmm, but for the right box, the G state shifted to rows 28-31, and the player was at rows 37-39. So there was room.
OK so the mechanic seems to be:
1. Move UP toward the box until it changes to G (creates a ghost copy shifted toward the player)
2. Press SPACE to toggle the ghost
3. Press LEFT to merge (when ghost is active)
For the bottom box, the ghost couldn't shift (blocked by player), so SPACE consumed it instead.
Now, for the current state (after reset):
- Player: rows 49-51, cols 32-35
- Bottom box: rows 36-39, cols 32-35
- Strip: rows 48, cols 32-35
The player is below the bottom box. If I move UP, the box changes to G. But the ghost can't shift down (player is in the way). So SPACE would consume the box.
For the right box and left box, the player is far away, so the ghost can shift. The sequence would be:
1. Move to the box's column
2. Move UP toward the box
3. SPACE to toggle ghost
4. LEFT to merge
But wait, looking at the right box interaction again:
- H25 (UP): box still c
- H26 (UP): box still c
- H27 (SPACE): box became G
So the box changed to G when SPACE was pressed, not when UP was pressed. The UP moves just brought the player closer.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- H25 (UP): box c, rows 24-27
- H26 (UP): box c, rows 24-27
- H27 (SPACE): box G, rows 28-31
So SPACE created the G state. The UP moves just positioned the player.
For the bottom box:
- H0: box c, rows 36-39
- H1 (UP): box c, rows 36-39
- H2 (UP): box G, rows 36-39
So the box changed to G after UP x2, not after SPACE. This is different from the right box where SPACE created the G state.
Wait, maybe I misread. Let me check H2 again.
Actually, looking at the earlier output:
"H2 (action: UP): color=G, pixels=12, b=[[36, 32], [36, 35], [39, 35], [39, 32]]"
So at H2, after UP, the box changed to G. This happened when the player moved close enough to the box.
For the right box:
"H25 (action: UP): color=c, pixels=12, b=[[24, 44], [24, 47], [27, 47], [27, 44]]"
"H26 (action: UP): color=c, pixels=12, b=[[24, 44], [24, 47], [27, 47], [27, 44]]"
"H27 (action: SPACE): color=c, pixels=12, b=[[24, 44], [24, 47], [27, 47], [27, 44]]"
"H27 (action: SPACE): color=G, pixels=12, b=[[28, 44], [28, 47], [31, 47], [31, 44]]"
So at H27, SPACE was pressed, and the box changed to G. The box stayed c during UP moves.
The difference: for the bottom box, the box changed to G when the player moved close (UP). For the right box, the box changed to G when SPACE was pressed.
Maybe the trigger depends on the distance. For the bottom box, the player was close enough that moving UP triggered the G state. For the right box, the player was farther away, so SPACE was needed.
Actually, I think the key is:
- When the player is close enough to the box (within some threshold), moving toward it changes its color to G.
- When the player is farther away, SPACE is needed to create the G state.
For the bottom box, the player started at rows 49-51, and the box was at rows 36-39. After UP x2, the player was at rows 47-49, still 8 rows from the box. But the box changed to G.
For the right box, the player was at rows 37-39 (after UP x2 from rows 41-43), and the box was at rows 24-27. The distance was 37-27 = 10 rows. The box stayed c.
Hmm, the distances are similar (8 vs 10 rows). Maybe the trigger is different.
Actually, I think the key difference is that for the bottom box, the player and box are in the SAME columns (32-35). For the right box, the player and box are in different columns initially, and the player moves RIGHT to align with the box.
Wait, but at H25-26, the player was at cols 44-47 (aligned with the right box), and the box stayed c. So column alignment alone doesn't trigger the G state.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the G state is triggered by:
1. For the bottom box: player is in the same column, and moves UP close enough → G state
2. For the right box: player is in the same column, and presses SPACE → G state
The difference might be the initial distance. For the bottom box, the player starts closer (rows 49-51 vs 36-39, distance 10 rows). For the right box, the player starts farther (rows 41-43 vs 24-27, distance 14 rows).
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just focus on what works:
- For the right box: UP x2, SPACE x3, LEFT → grew
- For the bottom box: UP x3, SPACE → consumed
The difference is that for the right box, SPACE was used to toggle the ghost, and LEFT merged. For the bottom box, SPACE consumed the box.
Now, the game has reset. I'm back at the initial state. I need to:
1. Grow the right box (UP x2, SPACE x3, LEFT)
2. Grow the left box (same sequence)
3. Handle the bottom box differently (maybe don't interact with it, or use a different sequence)
But wait, the game reset because I triggered GAME OVER. What caused the game over? Looking at the last action sequence: UP x5, LEFT x18. The player moved UP into the box area and then LEFT. Maybe moving into the box's area triggered game over.
Actually, looking at the result: "game_over: true" but "level_completed: false" and "done: false". So it's a game over (loss), not a level completion.
The game over was triggered by the player moving into a forbidden area or colliding with something. Looking at the animation, the player moved UP x5 (from rows 44-47 to rows 39-42) and then LEFT x18. At some point, the player must have collided with something.
Actually, looking at the current state image, the player is at rows 49-51, cols 32-35. The game reset the player to the initial position. So the game over was a reset, not a permanent loss.
Now I need to figure out the correct sequence to grow all three boxes. Let me focus on the right box first, since I know the sequence works:
1. Move RIGHT to align with right box (cols 44-47)
2. UP x2 to approach
3. SPACE x3 to toggle ghost
4. LEFT to merge
But I need to be careful not to trigger game over. The game over was triggered by moving UP x5, which put the player too close to the boxes.
Let me try the right box sequence first: RIGHT x12 (to cols 44-46), UP x2, SPACE x3, LEFT.
Actually wait, the player is at cols 32-35. The right box is at cols 44-47. To align, I need RIGHT x12 (32+12=44).
But the player is 4 cols wide (32-35), and the box is 4 cols wide (44-47). RIGHT x12 → cols 44-47, which is exactly the box's columns.
Let me try: RIGHT x12, UP x2, SPACE x3, LEFT.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: GAME RESET! Back to initial state. Player at rows 49-51, cols 32-35. Three boxes: right (24-27, 44-47), left (28-31, 16-19), bottom (36-39, 32-35). Key findings from right box: SPACE toggles box between c (original) and G (shifted) states. When box is in G state, LEFT merges them into a doubled box. The sequence was: UP x2, SPACE x3, LEFT → box doubled. Bottom box was consumed because SPACE extended the strip into it. Game over was triggered by moving too far UP.
Plan: Try the right box sequence: RIGHT x12 to align, UP x2, SPACE x3, LEFT to grow. Then repeat for left box.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Right box sequence: RIGHT x12 to align with box cols 44-47, UP x2, SPACE x3, LEFT
action(['RIGHT'] * 12 + ['UP', 'UP', 'SPACE', 'SPACE', 'SPACE', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
